ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pasta alla Genovese with pesto, burratina, olives, green beans, and pine nuts. FOODON_00002473 (pasta food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Pasta alla Genovese with pesto and accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Italian pasta preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of Genovese pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of pasta dressed with basil pesto, topped with burratina DOP, mozzarella, Taggiasche olives, green beans, and pine nuts.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with pesto sauce, cooked — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods and USDA SR Legacy pasta entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Pasta alla Genovese with burratina, pesto, Taggiasche olives, green beans, and pine nuts as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted-average composition of component foods: cooked pasta (USDA FDC #168930), basil pesto (USDA FDC #173944), mozzarella/burrata cheese (USDA FDC #170845), Taggiasche olives (approximated from USDA ripe olives FDC #169094), green beans cooked (USDA FDC #169961), and pine nuts (USDA FDC #170591). Approximate dish composition assumed: 55% pasta, 15% pesto, 15% cheese (burratina + mozzarella), 7% olives, 5% green beans, 3% pine nuts. Per-100g values derived from this weighted blend.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta, cooked al dente (wheat-based)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Pasta alla Genovese with pesto and burratina. GI of 45 is based on cooked wheat pasta (al dente) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which report GI values of 40–50 for cooked wheat pasta depending on cooking time and format. The addition of fat (pesto, cheese, olives) and protein (cheese) further lowers the effective GI. GI 45 is a conservative mid-range estimate for this pasta-dominant dish. GL per serving = (45 × 85.75 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 38.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from pesto, olives, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 39 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, choose al dente cooking, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from burratina DOP, mozzarella, and pesto; beneficial monounsaturated fat from olive oil and pine nuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 12.6 g saturated fat per serving alongside beneficial monounsaturated fats from olive oil and pine nuts; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of total saturated fat intake and consider sharing the portion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Genovese Pasta (Vegetarian) is an Italian pasta dish inspired by the classic Ligurian preparation known as Pasta alla Genovese, originating from Genoa in the Liguria region of northwestern Italy. The dish features cooked wheat pasta — traditionally trofie or linguine — dressed generously with authentic basil pesto (pesto alla Genovese), which is made from fresh basil, pine nuts, Parmigiano-Reggiano or Pecorino, garlic, and extra-virgin olive oil. This vegetarian version is enriched with Burratina DOP, a fresh Italian cheese with a creamy interior, along with mozzarella cheese, Taggiasche olives (small, mild Ligurian olives prized for their delicate flavour), cooked green beans, and toasted pine nuts. The dish is served warm or at room temperature and presents a rich, herbaceous, and creamy flavour profile with textural contrast from the olives, beans, and nuts. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from dairy, complex carbohydrates from pasta, and beneficial fats from olive oil and pine nuts. The sodium content is elevated due to pesto, olives, and cheese, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low to moderate (estimated GI 45) due to the pasta base, but the glycemic load per full restaurant serving is moderate at approximately 39. The dish is suitable for vegetarians but contains gluten, dairy, and tree nuts. It is not suitable for vegans or those with nut allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods using a weighted-average approach based on assumed dish composition. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked wheat pasta) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is well-defined from the menu description.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods (cooked pasta FDC #168930, basil pesto FDC #173944, mozzarella/burrata FDC #170845, ripe olives FDC #169094, cooked green beans FDC #169961, pine nuts FDC #170591) using an assumed weighted composition of approximately 55% pasta, 15% pesto, 15% cheese, 7% olives, 5% green beans, 3% pine nuts. 2. Serving size of 350 g is estimated for a restaurant main-course pasta portion; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-oil and dairy-based dish; no industrial trans fats are present in the component ingredients per USDA data. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1.0 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central for the component ingredients used in this estimation. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat pasta (al dente) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); the presence of fat and protein from pesto and cheese further moderates the glycaemic response. 6. The food_name 'Genovese (V)' from the input has been expanded to 'Genovese Pasta (Vegetarian)' for canonical clarity; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 7. This dish is distinct from the Neapolitan 'Ragù alla Genovese' (a slow-cooked onion and meat sauce); the Ligurian Pasta alla Genovese with pesto is the correct referent here. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component USDA sources and carry medium confidence.
